did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper explicitly states in the Abstract that 'machine learning techniques' were used to determine the sensitivity and specificity of differentiating skin cancers from normal skin, achieving ranges from about 78% to over 90%. Additionally, the text proposes using AI on lesion histopathology and mechanovibrational peak heights to differentiate metastatic potential.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a pilot study using vibrational optical coherence tomography (VOCT) to differentiate pigmented and non-pigmented melanomas. It includes specific sections for Methods (subjects, measurement, machine learning analysis, statistics) and Results (tables with statistical comparisons, sensitivity, and specificity data), indicating it is an original research study rather than a review.

title: Melanin Stacking Differences in Pigmented and Non-Pigmented Melanomas: Quantitative Differentiation between Pigmented and Non-Pigmented Melanomas Based on Light-Scattering Properties
brief_summary: This pilot study utilized vibrational optical coherence tomography (VOCT) to non-invasively differentiate between pigmented and non-pigmented melanomas based on their light-scattering properties and mechanovibrational peaks. The researchers analyzed resonant frequency peaks, specifically noting that pigmented melanomas exhibited larger 80 Hz peaks and smaller 250 Hz peaks compared to non-pigmented lesions, indicating higher melanin packing densities. Machine learning techniques, optimized via logistic regression, were applied to mechanovibrational data to assess diagnostic performance. The study achieved sensitivity and specificity ranges of approximately 78% to over 90% for differentiating skin cancers from normal skin. The results suggest that quantitative VOCT analysis combined with AI could enhance the differentiation of melanocytic lesions and potentially predict metastatic potential.

lesion_types_studied.details: n = 18; studied in vivo/excised biopsies; compared to non-pigmented melanomas and normal skin; used for differentiation based on melanin stacking and mechanovibrational peaks.
n = 55; studied in vivo/excised biopsies; compared to pigmented melanomas and normal skin; used for differentiation based on melanin stacking and mechanovibrational peaks.
n = 80; studied in vivo and in excised skin cancer biopsies; used as control for differentiating skin cancers.
Data taken from a recently published paper [1]; used for machine learning comparison of sensitivity and specificity against normal skin.
Data taken from a recently published paper [1]; used for machine learning comparison of sensitivity and specificity against normal skin.
Mentioned in the context of previous work [1] showing fingerprints significantly different from BCC, SCC, and melanoma; not part of the current study's experimental cohort.

all_task_types.target: Differentiation of skin cancers (pigmented melanoma, non-pigmented melanoma, BCC, SCC) from normal skin.
all_task_types.details: The study used machine learning techniques (optimized via logistic regression) to classify skin lesions. The primary task was differentiating pigmented and non-pigmented melanomas from normal skin, as well as comparing melanoma, BCC, and SCC against normal skin. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity and specificity metrics (ranging from 78% to over 90%).

datasets_used.name: VOCT Pilot Study Cohort (Primary)
datasets_used.description: A pilot study cohort consisting of normal skin and melanoma biopsies studied using vibrational optical coherence tomography (VOCT). Normal skin was studied in vivo and in excised biopsies. Melanoma biopsies were collected from patients undergoing excisional therapies.

use.training: Data from this cohort was used to calculate sensitivity and specificity for differentiating skin cancers from normal skin using machine learning techniques optimized via logistic regression.
use.testing: Data from this cohort was used to calculate sensitivity and specificity for differentiating skin cancers from normal skin using machine learning techniques optimized via logistic regression.
use.other: Used to compare resonant frequency peaks and pixel intensity between pigmented and non-pigmented melanomas and normal skin.

datasets_used.name: External Reference Dataset [1]
datasets_used.description: Data from a previously published paper [1] containing mechanovibrational data for basal cell carcinomas (BCCs) and squamous cell carcinomas (SCCs).

use.training: Data was inputted into the machine learning algorithm alongside the primary cohort data to compare sensitivity and specificity against normal skin.
use.testing: Data was used to calculate sensitivity and specificity for BCC and SCC diagnosis compared to normal skin.
use.other: Used for comparative analysis of machine learning results for BCC and SCC.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the resonant frequencies of normalized peak heights of normal skin and pigmented and non-pigmented melanoma, and to calculate p-values for statistical differences in pixel intensity between non-pigmented and pigmented melanomas.
To determine statistical significance for the comparisons performed using the Student's t-test.
To optimize the accuracy of machine learning algorithms for differentiating skin cancers from normal skin.
To evaluate the diagnostic performance of the machine learning models in differentiating skin cancers (BCC, SCC, Melanoma) from normal skin.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in the resonant frequencies of normalized peak heights (or pixel intensity) between the compared groups (e.g., normal skin vs. pigmented/non-pigmented melanomas, or pigmented vs. non-pigmented melanomas).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a difference in the resonant frequencies of normalized peak heights (or pixel intensity) between the compared groups, specifically testing for the hypothesis that pigmented and non-pigmented melanomas can be quantitatively differentiated.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: P-values less than 0.05 were considered statistically significant, indicating that the observed differences in resonant frequency peaks and pixel intensity between normal skin and melanomas, as well as between pigmented and non-pigmented melanomas, were statistically significant.

VOCT_Pilot_Cohort_Normal_Skin_vs_Melanom.metric: Resonant Frequency Peak Heights (Normalized Weighted Displacement)
VOCT_Pilot_Cohort_Normal_Skin_vs_Melanom.notes: Non-pigmented and pigmented melanomas can be differentiated from normal skin based on the 80 and 250 Hz peak heights. Pigmented melanomas have larger 80 Hz peaks and smaller 250 Hz peaks than non-pigmented cancers.

Pixel_Intensity_Comparison.observation: Pigmented melanomas scatter more light than non-pigmented melanomas, indicating different melanin stacking.

VOCT_Pilot_Cohort_Pigmented_vs_Non_Pigme.notes: The pixel depth at which pigmented lesions scatter infrared light is greater than that observed for non-pigmented lesions, suggesting greater melanin packing density in pigmented melanomas. Pigmented melanoma average thickness: 256 µm (SD 29.4 µm). Non-pigmented melanoma average thickness: 277 µm (SD 30.1 µm).

Machine_Learning_Classification_Normal_S.notes: Results calculated from mechanovibrational data and machine learning techniques. Data for BCC and SCC taken from a previously published paper [1]. The study reports that VOCT data can differentiate between different types of skin cancers non-invasively with between 78% and 92% accuracy.

architecture_details.description: Machine learning algorithms were implemented and compared, with accuracy optimized using a logistic regression model. The specific implementation details of the logistic regression (e.g., regularization, solver) are not reported.

training_details.dataset_source: Mechanovibrational data from VOCT measurements on normal skin, pigmented melanomas, non-pigmented melanomas, basal cell carcinomas (BCC), and squamous cell carcinomas (SCC). Data for BCC and SCC was taken from a previously published paper [1].
training_details.input_features: Resonant frequency peaks (50, 80, 130, 250 Hz), pixel intensity, and light scattering depth data derived from VOCT.

preprocessing_techniques: Normalization of weighted displacement values for resonant frequency peaks
Conversion of resonant frequencies into elastic modulus values using a calibration equation
Histopathological examination and comparison with resonant frequency peak distributions
conclusions: Pigmented and non-pigmented melanomas can be quantitatively differentiated from each other and from normal skin using vibrational optical coherence tomography (VOCT) based on mechanovibrational peak heights, specifically the 80 Hz and 250 Hz peaks.
Pigmented melanomas exhibit larger 80 Hz peaks and smaller 250 Hz peaks compared to non-pigmented melanomas.
Infrared light penetration depth analysis indicates that melanin in pigmented melanomas has higher packing densities than in non-pigmented lesions, as evidenced by greater light scattering.

Machine learning techniques optimized via logistic regression can differentiate skin cancers (basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanoma) from normal skin with sensitivities and specificities ranging from approximately 78% to over 90%.

The study proposes that integrating artificial intelligence with both lesion histopathology and mechanovibrational peak heights may provide even higher specificity and sensitivity for differentiating the metastatic potential of melanocytic lesions.
important_remarks: The study is a pilot study with a small sample size (n=18 pigmented melanomas, n=55 non-pigmented melanomas, n=80 normal skin), limiting statistical power and generalizability.

Data for Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) was sourced from a previously published paper [1] rather than collected in the current study, introducing potential heterogeneity in data acquisition methods.
The study lacks external validation; results are derived from a single cohort and do not assess performance on independent, multi-center datasets.

The machine learning model (logistic regression) was optimized for accuracy but specific hyperparameters, cross-validation strategies, or train/test splits are not reported, raising concerns about potential overfitting or data leakage.

The paper acknowledges that additional data is needed to differentiate pigmented BCC from melanocytic lesions, indicating a current limitation in diagnostic specificity for pigmented non-melanoma skin cancers.
The study proposes using AI on histopathology and mechanovibrational data to predict metastatic potential, but this is a proposal/hypothesis not tested or validated within this work.

No external funding was received, and while conflicts of interest are declared as none, the authors are affiliated with OptoVibronex, LLC, which may present a bias regarding the commercialization of the VOCT technology.
The statistical analysis used unpaired one-tailed Student’s t-tests, which may be less conservative than two-tailed tests, potentially inflating significance.
